title: Ruling Out
alt: We were able to replicate and confirm prior authors' detection of a moon orbiting the Earth with high confidence.
transcript:
[Cueball is talking to Megan.]
Cueball: So far our astronomy group has published studies ruling out the existence of Earthlike stars, exoplanets in our solar system, habitable-zone quasars, stars with subsurface oceans, and tectonically active black holes.

[Caption below the panel:]
Science got way easier when we realized you were allowed to do studies just to rule stuff out.
explanation:
Most science studies are intended to discover new knowledge. In astronomy, the goal is often to find different types of objects in space, or learn how astronomical objects are formed and behave. But often from studying things that exist, we also learn about limits of the kinds of things that can exist; when this happens, we say that we've "ruled out" the excluded phenomena.

Cueball lists five impossible objects his team has ruled out:

* "Earthlike stars": A play on "Earth-like planets" which scientists are very interested in finding. The Earth is (probably) not a , hence stars cannot be Earthlike (even other similarities (such as habitability and liquid water) are extremely hard to achieve, due to high gravity and sometimes very high temperature).
: Searches for both  and  go unabated, with various near matches found.

* "Exoplanets in our solar system": s are by definition not in our solar system.
: Planets in our solar system (even ) are unaffected, as is the  around other stars, with conclusive evidence of both.

* "Habitable-zone quasars": s in the s of stars are only theoretically feasible for relatively small s with active s  in a star's habitable zone, visible from the Earth and brighter than the Sun, because of the technical criteria for classifying them in terms of their  relative to that of their galaxy. None such have ever been observed. While typical galaxies usually have only one quasar in their center, merging galaxies often have two far apart. Perhaps in 4-5 billion years, when the  merges with our , its microquasar might qualify, but that is extremely unlikely.
:While not certain, habitable zones around some quasars have not been ruled out.

* "Stars with subsurface oceans": Because the temperatures inside stars are higher than that which can support the existence of liquids as we understand them, stars cannot have subsurface oceans. After many billions of years, a  will cool to the point where it no longer emits significant heat or light, becoming a , eventually cooling to the point where it might develop subsurface liquids. However, the universe is not old enough for any black dwarfs to exist yet, and sufficiently cool black dwarfs might not even be considered stars, but rather s.
: The possibility of subsurface oceans within various planets and moons is an , and was previously mentioned 10 comics ago in 2773: Planetary Scientist.

* "Tectonically active black holes": Black holes do not have s, so they cannot be tectonically active.
: There are theories that neutron stars can exhibit  (as some of the more typical rocky bodies certainly do), but the physics of the 'inside' of a black hole are thought to involve  incompatible with any form of geology, and cannot be observed anyway – it is believed that the only externally-observable properties of black holes are mass, electric charge, and angular momentum, poetically called the ''.

The joke is that you don't actually have to study anything to come to these almost patently obvious conclusions. The objects are contradictory by their very names ("planets that are both inside and not inside the solar system") so the fact they can't exist would be obvious to anyone who understands the basic definitions, much less a researcher in an astronomy-related field. The counter-proposals would need far more effort to even justify them as valid theories, by common understanding, and greater still to try to observe any supporting proof.

Some studies are also done to confirm the results of previous studies, to ensure that the conclusions were not mistaken or a fluke. The title text describes a study that was done to confirm the existence of a moon orbiting Earth, even though any sighted person can walk outside and see the Moon, the existence of the Moon has been known for at least as long as humanity has existed, and the fact that it orbits the Earth has been assumed or known for upwards of 3,000 years. The ancient Greeks and Babylonians, for example, thought that the Moon orbited the Earth, though they lacked a detailed physical understanding of the system (they also believed, erroneously, that ).  (c. 500–428 BC) is credited with the correct explanation of lunar eclipses, and reportedly was the first to explain that the Moon shines due to reflected light from the Sun. However, it was not until the work of  in the 16th century that a detailed and accurate model of the Moon's orbit around the Earth was developed.  Regardless, at this stage, a study to confirm the validity of Copernican orbits would contribute nothing to the scientific process, much less a study confirming the mere existence of the Moon.
